archetype.label: Upstream Advisory / Convening
archetype.description: Advises, funds, or convenes education operators — foundations, networks, consultancies

flags: Directly causes revenue loss, churn, or failed deals
Creates friction for teams beyond the owning function
Would surface in a board report or investor due diligence
Data inputs are structured/digital (not tacit knowledge)
Augments existing workflow (doesn't require behavior change)
No regulatory barrier specific to student/minor data
Value demonstrable in < 2 weeks (fast feedback loop)
No change management beyond the direct user

painPoints.label: Senior Staff Bottleneck in Proposal Writing
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: proposals requiring Director or Principal-level authorship because junior staff lack the contextual authority or relationship credibility to lead client-facing scoping conversations; time lost when senior advisors must draft or extensively rewrite proposals to meet quality thresholds; opportunity cost of senior time diverted from billable delivery. EXCLUDES: capacity constraints in delivery phases, general workload management, recruitment gaps.

pain_salience: revenue-loss
felt-weekly
board-visible

painPoints.rationale: High-frequency senior time drain with clear AI drafting augmentation path; value is fast to demonstrate via side-by-side draft quality, but adoption requires senior trust in AI output, moderating ease somewhat.

painPoints.label: Scope Inflation During Client Needs Conversations
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: client expectations expanding beyond what the proposed budget can support during initial scoping calls; difficulty saying no to influential government or funder clients whose goodwill is commercially important; proposals that grow in ambition between first conversation and contract signature, compressing margins or forcing unpaid overruns. EXCLUDES: scope creep during delivery (managed under Engagement Management), budget negotiation failures post-contract.

pain_salience: Directly compresses margins or forces unpaid overruns
Felt in most client-facing scoping conversations

painPoints.rationale: The core pain is a relational and power dynamic with influential clients — no AI tool makes it easier to say no to a funder. Scoping checklists help marginally; the root cause is political, not informational.

painPoints.label: High Unpaid Investment in Lost Proposals
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: significant senior hours invested in scoping and proposal writing for bids that are not awarded; inability to accurately predict win rates across government, foundation, and multilateral clients; no mechanism to recover proposal costs when the client selects a lower-cost competitor or changes priorities. EXCLUDES: grant application costs (Partnerships & Fundraising), retainer or repeat client proposals with high win certainty.

pain_salience: revenue-loss
Felt with every major proposal cycle
board-visible

painPoints.rationale: AI can reduce drafting cost per proposal by reusing prior content intelligently and help score bid quality before investing senior time, but cannot fix the fundamental unpredictability of government procurement decisions.

painPoints.label: Difficulty Benchmarking Day Rates Across Client Types
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: different rate expectations from government ministries vs. foundations vs. multilaterals creating internal inconsistency; pressure from price-sensitive government clients to match cheaper consulting alternatives; lack of market data on what comparable advisory organisations charge for equivalent expertise, leading to systematic under-pricing. EXCLUDES: contract negotiation for already-agreed budgets, grant budget construction (Partnerships & Fundraising).

pain_salience: revenue-loss
Creates internal inconsistency across client types

painPoints.rationale: Market rate benchmarking for niche advisory in low-income country education reform is fundamentally a data availability problem; AI cannot synthesise data that does not exist in structured form.

painPoints.label: Overhead of Bespoke Theory of Change per Engagement
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: each proposal requiring a fresh engagement-level theory of change (the logic linking advisory inputs to this engagement's specific intended outcomes) drafted from scratch, even when the advisory approach is standardised across similar engagements; internal reviewers disagreeing on required ToC format or depth — e.g. whether a narrative logic model suffices or a full logframe is needed — triggering revision cycles that consume days before submission; time spent in senior sign-off on ToC logic displacing time available for substantive proposal content. EXCLUDES: organisation-level or programme-level ToC development for impact tracking (Monitoring, Evaluation & Learning); early scoping conversations where a ToC is sketched informally but not yet a formal proposal deliverable; impact measurement frameworks built post-contract.

pain_salience: felt-weekly
Creates revision cycles that displace substantive proposal work
build_complexity_inv: Value demonstrable in first use — AI-drafted ToC vs. blank page

painPoints.rationale: ToC drafting is a strong AI fit — structured inputs, formulaic output, fast feedback on quality. The pain (senior time in revision cycles) is real and frequent, making this a high-value, easy-to-build target.

painPoints.label: Stakeholder Access Delays Compressing Diagnostic Timelines
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: government officials or school leaders unavailable for interviews during the agreed diagnostic window; rescheduling cascades that push fieldwork past the deadline, reducing time for synthesis; client-side gatekeeping that limits access to the officials who hold the most relevant knowledge. EXCLUDES: delays caused by the advisory team's own scheduling failures, broader engagement timeline slippage.

pain_salience: Compresses diagnostic timeline, risking quality
Felt during every fieldwork phase
build_complexity_inv: Standard scheduling tools already exist for the mechanical part

painPoints.rationale: This is a coordination and political access problem, not an information processing problem. AI tools do not resolve government official availability or gatekeeper dynamics; existing calendar tools already address mechanical coordination.

painPoints.label: Inconsistent or Absent Administrative Data in Client Systems
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: government education data systems that are incomplete, outdated, or inaccessible; needing to reconstruct basic system facts (enrolment, teacher counts, budget flows) from fragmented sources; time lost validating conflicting figures from different ministries or departments within the same client organisation. EXCLUDES: independent research data collection for publishable studies (Research & Knowledge Production), MEL data collection for impact tracking.

pain_salience: Directly delays diagnostic delivery
Felt in virtually every LMIC engagement
Would surface as a risk flag in engagement scoping and reporting
build_complexity_inv: Value demonstrable in a single diagnostic context

painPoints.rationale: AI can accelerate triangulation of fragmented sources and flag inconsistencies, but cannot conjure data that does not exist; the bottleneck is data absence more than synthesis speed, limiting impact ceiling.

painPoints.label: Synthesis Bottleneck After Fieldwork Phase
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: one advisor facing weeks of interview notes, policy documents, and workshop outputs that must be synthesised into a structured problem statement within a compressed engagement timeline — typically 3-5 days where the volume of material warrants 2 weeks; key insights being missed or inadequately evidenced because synthesis is rushed; diagnostic quality varying significantly depending on the seniority and analytical capacity of the single advisor left to process the material. EXCLUDES: the absence of a documented synthesis methodology as an organisational-level concern (Advisory Practice & Methodology Development); writing up final recommendations after synthesis is complete (Strategy Development & Recommendations); publication of diagnostic findings (Communications & Publishing).

pain_salience: Directly affects diagnostic quality and timeline (revenue risk if deliverable is late or weak)
cross-functional-friction
Felt in every diagnostic engagement
Quality variance is visible to clients and would surface in delivery QA

painPoints.rationale: The clearest high-value AI fit in this domain: large volume of digital text, time-pressured synthesis task, demonstrable quality uplift, direct impact on deliverable quality and senior time. Adoption risk is low — augments rather than replaces judgment.

painPoints.label: Client Resistance to Diagnostic Findings at Validation Stage
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: senior government officials disputing diagnostic conclusions that are politically uncomfortable; pressure to soften problem statements to preserve relationships; advisory team having to re-run validation workshops when initial findings are rejected, consuming budget and time. EXCLUDES: resistance to strategic recommendations (Strategy Development & Recommendations), post-engagement disagreement on impact.

pain_salience: Triggers costly re-work consuming budget and time
Felt during validation workshops across engagements
Can derail relationship and engagement outcome

painPoints.rationale: The pain is real but the root cause is power dynamics between advisors and senior officials. AI cannot change who holds political authority; pre-validating findings against political feasibility is something advisory teams already do informally.

painPoints.label: Repeated Diagnostic Work Across Similar Client Contexts
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: advisors beginning a new engagement in a country or sector the organisation has previously served, and rebuilding system maps, data inventories, and contextual analyses from scratch because prior diagnostic material (interview summaries, sector overviews, gap analyses, analytical frameworks applied) is stored in individual advisors' personal files or email, not indexed or searchable by the broader team; time lost reconstructing context that was already assembled within the last 1-3 years; institutional memory of system dynamics and political context locked in the heads of advisors who worked on prior engagements. EXCLUDES: the difficulty of extracting case study material for internal reuse when client confidentiality prevents documentation of specifics (Advisory Practice & Methodology Development — case-study-extraction); methodology development and codification of practice approaches (Advisory Practice & Methodology Development); knowledge dissemination to the field (Communications & Publishing).

pain_salience: Directly wastes senior time rebuilding prior context
Felt at the start of every new engagement in known sectors
Would surface in utilisation and efficiency tracking

painPoints.rationale: A strong RAG use case — existing digital material locked in file systems, high-frequency retrieval need, clear time-saving on engagement startup. Confidentiality management adds complexity but is solvable with access controls.

painPoints.label: Gap Between Available Evidence and Client's Specific Context
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: global evidence base not translating cleanly to the client's system constraints (fiscal headroom, political economy, implementation capacity); advisors spending significant time adapting generic frameworks to context rather than applying them directly; risk of over-relying on evidence from high-income contexts for low-income country clients. EXCLUDES: gaps in the global evidence base itself (Research & Knowledge Production), client unwillingness to accept evidence-based recommendations.

pain_salience: Affects recommendation quality and client trust
Felt in every engagement requiring evidence synthesis
Risk of wrong-context evidence use is a reputational risk
build_complexity_inv: Value demonstrable with a test case

painPoints.rationale: AI can accelerate evidence retrieval and provide structured adaptation frameworks, but the core judgment — whether evidence from one context applies to another — requires deep expertise AI does not reliably possess; risk of false confidence is real.

painPoints.label: Recommendation Iteration Cycles Exceeding Scoped Budget
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: clients requesting revision rounds on strategy documents and recommendations reports that go beyond what the contract scope anticipated, treating iterative refinement as a normal part of the engagement even after deliverables have been formally submitted; pressure on advisors to absorb additional revision cycles without raising a contract amendment in order to preserve the relationship with an influential client; senior advisor days consumed by repeated restructuring or re-drafting of deliverables that were expected to be in final review; the specific friction of clients who reopen the options set or reframe the problem after a strategy document has been accepted in draft. EXCLUDES: legitimate revision cycles that fall within the scoped contract deliverables and were budgeted as part of the engagement design; scope changes that are genuinely new client requests requiring formal contract amendment (Engagement Scoping & Proposal Development for pre-contract; Engagement Management & Client Relationship for post-contract amendments); MEL feedback loops on strategy implementation.

pain_salience: Directly creates unpaid senior advisor days
Felt across most strategy deliverable cycles
revenue-loss

painPoints.rationale: The pain is a client relationship and contracting problem, not an information or drafting problem. AI cannot make it easier to raise a contract amendment with an influential government client; the root cause is unaddressed by tooling.

painPoints.label: Recommendations Constrained by Unstated Political Constraints
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: strategy options that are technically sound but politically unfeasible — known to the client but not disclosed during scoping; discovering mid-engagement that certain reform pathways are off-limits, requiring the team to rebuild the options analysis; advisors unable to surface politically sensitive constraints in workshop settings with mixed seniority client groups. EXCLUDES: implementation barriers that emerge after the engagement closes, advocacy for policy change (Communications & Publishing).

pain_salience: Causes mid-engagement rebuild of options analysis
Felt in a significant share of government advisory engagements
Can result in unrecoverable engagement failure
build_complexity_inv: Value demonstrable on a test engagement context

painPoints.rationale: A political economy pre-screening tool is plausible and useful, but requires high-quality country and actor context data that is often tacit; impact is limited to flagging known patterns, not discovering actively undisclosed constraints.

painPoints.label: Co-Design Workshops Consuming Disproportionate Senior Time
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: senior advisors required to facilitate all strategic co-design sessions because junior staff lack the authority to hold the room with government or funder counterparts; preparation time for workshops (design, materials, pre-reads) often exceeding facilitation time itself; difficulty running multiple co-design engagements in parallel when facilitation demand concentrates on 2-3 people. EXCLUDES: convening events for field-building (Events & Convening), capacity building workshops delivered to client staff (Implementation Support & Technical Assistance).

pain_salience: Directly caps parallel delivery capacity on senior bottleneck
Felt every time co-design workshops are scheduled
board-visible
build_complexity_inv: Value demonstrable — prep time reduction visible immediately

painPoints.rationale: AI can significantly reduce workshop preparation burden but cannot substitute for the senior presence required to hold the room with government counterparts. Scored on prep augmentation only, which is real but not the primary pain.

painPoints.label: Inconsistent Deliverable Quality Across Advisory Teams
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: strategy documents and recommendations reports varying significantly in structure, analytical rigour, evidence use, and persuasiveness depending on which advisor leads drafting — visible inconsistency that clients or funders notice across engagements or when comparing outputs from different advisory teams; absence of shared templates, style standards, or quality rubrics for strategy deliverables, meaning each advisor applies personal standards that may be substantially different from colleagues'; the experience of a senior reviewer catching structural or analytical quality problems close to the client submission deadline — the failure mode being that no earlier checkpoint caught the issue, requiring rushed rework. EXCLUDES: the QA review process itself and its scheduling (managed as a distinct engagement lifecycle step under Engagement Management & Client Relationship); gaps in advisory methodology or analytical frameworks that no template could remedy (Advisory Practice & Methodology Development); client feedback on deliverables after submission.

pain_salience: Client-visible quality inconsistency is a reputational and retention risk
Felt across the full advisory team output
board-visible
build_complexity_inv: Value demonstrable in first use — AI critique of a draft vs. no critique

painPoints.rationale: AI-assisted quality rubric review (structure, evidence use, internal logic, persuasiveness) is a strong fit: digital text input, structured rubric output, fast feedback loop, directly addresses the failure mode of issues surfacing only at final review.

painPoints.label: Difficulty Maintaining Embedded Advisor Continuity
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: advisors assigned to embedded TA roles being pulled to higher-urgency engagements, leaving the client without consistent support; high travel requirements for in-country embedding straining a small team; advisor transitions mid-engagement causing relationship disruption and knowledge loss for the client. EXCLUDES: material knowledge loss when advisors depart — toolkit and template inaccessibility (Toolkits and Templates Not Reused Across Engagements, in this branch); recruitment and retention strategy for advisory staff (People & Talent); advisor wellbeing and burnout from travel demands (People & Talent); travel cost policy and reimbursement (Corporate Operations).

pain_salience: Disrupts client relationships and trust mid-engagement
Felt with small-team capacity constraints on every parallel engagement
Would surface in client satisfaction and engagement continuity tracking
adoption_likelihood: Root cause is team capacity and travel logistics, not information or tooling

painPoints.rationale: An AI knowledge-handover tool could reduce transition friction marginally, but the core problem is staffing and availability — AI cannot staff a vacancy or reduce travel demands. Very low AI leverage.

painPoints.label: Capacity Built in Government Teams Not Retained Due to Staff Turnover
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: government counterparts trained or coached during TA engagements leaving their roles before capacity is institutionalised; advisory team needing to re-invest in relationship and knowledge transfer with replacement officials; TA engagements effectively starting over when a key ministry champion departs. EXCLUDES: impact evaluation of capacity outcomes (Monitoring, Evaluation & Learning), advocacy for civil service reform.

pain_salience: Forces re-investment of advisor time with replacement officials
Felt across most multi-year government TA engagements
Would surface as a delivery risk in engagement reporting

painPoints.rationale: The root problem is government civil service turnover — AI cannot fix that. Digital knowledge transfer materials can reduce re-onboarding burden, but this is standard e-learning capability, not a distinctive AI opportunity.

painPoints.label: Toolkits and Templates Not Reused Across Engagements
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: advisors recreating implementation guidance documents for each engagement rather than adapting prior versions; no indexed library of TA materials (templates, checklists, facilitation guides, guidance notes for client-facing use) accessible to the full team; materials developed in one country context not adapted for use in similar contexts served by different advisors. Toolkits and templates are operational delivery-support materials distinct from analytical methodology — a facilitation guide for a capacity-building workshop is a toolkit in scope here. EXCLUDES: advisory methodology codification — diagnostic protocols, problem-framing frameworks, and analytical approaches (Advisory Practice & Methodology Development); publication of toolkits externally (Communications & Publishing).

pain_salience: Wastes advisor time recreating materials
Felt at the start of each TA engagement
Compounds systematically over many engagements

painPoints.rationale: A strong library-plus-adaptation use case: existing digital materials, high-frequency adaptation need, clear time saving, standard RAG pattern. Parallel to diagnostic reuse failure but for operational TA materials.

painPoints.label: Scope Boundary Erosion Between Advisory Support and Direct Execution
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: clients pushing advisors to take on execution tasks rather than advising on them (drafting government communications, directly managing sub-contractors, filling operational gaps in ministry teams); advisors accepting execution tasks to preserve relationships, blurring accountability and increasing liability; internal confusion about when TA transitions from advisory to programme management requiring different contracting and governance. EXCLUDES: requests for high-frequency advisory guidance that remain advisory in nature but exceed scoped volume (Unpredictable Demand for Adaptive Guidance Between Milestones, in this branch); the organisation deliberately choosing to directly implement programmes (out of archetype scope); legal and contracting risk management (Corporate Operations).

pain_salience: Creates liability and unpaid work when advisors absorb execution tasks
Felt in engagements with underpowered client counterparts
Would surface in risk management and legal review
build_complexity_inv: Possible: scope boundary classifier for task categorisation, but limited leverage

painPoints.rationale: The problem is a relational and contracting discipline challenge — advisors accept execution tasks because refusing risks the relationship. An AI scope-boundary checker could flag when tasks cross the line, but the decision is always human and relational.

painPoints.label: Unpredictable Demand for Adaptive Guidance Between Milestones
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: clients contacting advisors with urgent troubleshooting requests outside the scoped engagement rhythm (off-cycle calls, last-minute brief requests, emergency strategy pivots) — where the nature of the work remains advisory (advising, recommending, sense-checking) but the volume and timing are unforeseeable; advisory team unable to plan capacity when reactive TA demand cannot be predicted; under-scoped contracts that priced reactive advisory support as zero creating uncompensated overruns. EXCLUDES: requests for advisors to take on execution tasks rather than advising (Scope Boundary Erosion Between Advisory Support and Direct Execution, in this branch); engagement scope changes requiring formal contract amendment (Engagement Management & Client Relationship); crisis communications (Communications & Publishing).

pain_salience: Directly creates uncompensated overruns
Felt with high-touch government clients between formal milestones
board-visible
build_complexity_inv: Value demonstrable on a real off-cycle request

painPoints.rationale: AI-assisted drafting of reactive advisory responses could reduce per-request senior time burden, but the highest-value reactive guidance is highly contextual and relationship-sensitive, limiting automation depth.

painPoints.label: TA Engagement Undermined by Insufficient Client Implementation Capacity
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: client organisations lacking the budget authority, staffing headcount, or political mandate to act on the implementation plan the advisory team is supporting; advisors providing technically sound TA into a system that cannot absorb or execute it; engagements designed around an optimistic client readiness assumption that proves false mid-engagement — e.g., the ministry official who signed off on the implementation plan has no budget line to fund it; advisory team caught between honest assessment of client capacity and the relationship cost of saying the client is not ready. EXCLUDES: staff churn in government counterpart teams after capacity has been built (Capacity Built in Government Teams Not Retained Due to Staff Turnover); the advisory team's own delivery capacity constraints (Difficulty Maintaining Embedded Advisor Continuity); TA that fails due to client resistance to the advisory approach rather than implementation capacity (Engagement Management & Client Relationship).

pain_salience: Causes engagement failure and relationship damage when absorption capacity is insufficient
Felt in engagements designed around optimistic client readiness assumptions
board-visible
build_complexity_inv: Value demonstrable on a test engagement context

painPoints.rationale: A structured AI-assisted client readiness diagnostic at scoping could surface capacity gaps before investment is made; useful and buildable, but the hard part is that weak clients often cannot or will not disclose their true capacity constraints.

painPoints.label: Quality Assurance Reviews Concentrated at Deadline
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: (a) QA checkpoints that exist but are positioned too close to the client submission deadline to allow substantive revision — senior sign-off arriving when changes are no longer feasible; (b) QA checkpoints that are absent or undefined, with quality review relying entirely on ad hoc senior reading rather than structured gates; junior advisors discovering QA expectations only when deliverables are nearly complete, requiring rushed rework; QA treated as a formality rather than a genuine quality gate. EXCLUDES: the content quality of advisory deliverables themselves — inconsistent structure, rigour, or persuasiveness across advisors (Strategy Development & Recommendations: Inconsistent Deliverable Quality Across Advisory Teams); methodology gaps identified during delivery (Advisory Practice & Methodology Development); client feedback received after delivery to the client.

pain_salience: Directly causes rushed rework close to deadline
Felt on every major deliverable cycle
Structural QA failure visible to clients when quality slips through

painPoints.rationale: Automated mid-cycle QA directly addresses the described failure mode — it creates an earlier checkpoint without requiring new process design. High fit, low build complexity, fast time-to-value.

painPoints.label: Project Management Overhead Falling on Senior Advisors
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: no dedicated project manager role in small teams, so workplan tracking, milestone monitoring, and risk logging falls to the lead advisor; time spent on administrative coordination (scheduling, tracking deliverables, chasing inputs) reducing time available for substantive advisory work; inconsistent project management discipline across engagements depending on individual advisor habits. EXCLUDES: financial reporting and invoicing (Corporate Operations), HR coordination (People & Talent).

pain_salience: Directly reduces billable hours for senior advisors
Felt continuously on every active engagement
Would surface in utilisation and efficiency analysis

painPoints.rationale: AI-assisted project status tracking and automated progress summaries reduce admin overhead, but dedicated PM tools already address much of this; the distinctive AI value is synthesis and risk-flagging from unstructured engagement notes.

painPoints.label: Scope Change Negotiation Straining Client Relationships
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: difficulty raising contract amendments with clients who view changes as advisory team failures; advisors absorbing out-of-scope work to avoid relationship friction, creating unpaid overruns; internal disagreement on when a client request constitutes a scope change vs. a reasonable extension of service. EXCLUDES: initial scope definition (Engagement Scoping & Proposal Development), billing disputes (Corporate Operations).

pain_salience: Creates unpaid overruns and relationship friction
Felt in most multi-milestone engagements
build_complexity_inv: Possible: scope drift detection from deliverable comparison, but limited leverage

painPoints.rationale: A scope-drift detection tool is marginally useful, but the real barrier is relational — advisors know they are absorbing out-of-scope work and choose to do so. AI does not change the incentive structure.

painPoints.label: Lessons-Learned Capture Skipped at Engagement Closeout
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: closeout documentation deprioritised when advisors are immediately reallocated to the next engagement; lessons remaining in individuals' heads or personal files rather than captured in any shared format; no structured retrospective process at engagement end, so the same contextual mistakes and client dynamics recur across engagements and advisors. The lessons captured here are engagement-specific records (what happened in this context, with this client, on this problem) intended to inform future advisors working on similar engagements — distinct from methodology codification, which abstracts patterns across engagements into generalisable frameworks. EXCLUDES: abstracting engagement patterns into generalisable diagnostic or methodological frameworks (Advisory Practice & Methodology Development); impact evaluation and outcome assessment (Monitoring, Evaluation & Learning); client-facing closeout and handover documentation — leaving the client adequately equipped after the engagement ends (see Engagement Closeout and Client Handover Gap).

pain_salience: Causes recurring mistakes across engagements
Felt at every closeout — systematic not one-off
build_complexity_inv: Value demonstrable in first use — auto-generated lessons draft vs. blank page

painPoints.rationale: Auto-generating a structured lessons-learned draft from engagement materials at closeout directly addresses the failure mode — the effort barrier is removed, making capture more likely even under time pressure.

painPoints.label: Client Relationship Continuity Risk from Advisor Departures
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: engagements where the client relationship is owned by a single advisor; departure or extended leave of that advisor creating relationship vacuum and trust deficit with the client; no structured process for relationship handover or secondary relationship building during the engagement. EXCLUDES: recruitment and retention strategy (People & Talent), new business development relationships (Partnerships & Fundraising).

pain_salience: Departure risk for single-relationship engagements is a revenue and retention risk
Felt on every engagement with a single relationship owner
Would surface in client retention risk tracking
build_complexity_inv: Value demonstrable on a real handover scenario

painPoints.rationale: AI-generated relationship briefing documents reduce handover friction when an advisor departs, but the trust dimension of client relationships cannot be transferred via a document — impact is real but limited.

painPoints.label: Inconsistent Client Progress Communication During Delivery
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: advisors providing progress updates to clients ad hoc and informally rather than through structured reporting cadences; clients developing anxiety, distrust, or unrealistic expectations during intensive work phases due to silence from the advisory team; inconsistent reporting formats across engagements and advisors making it hard for clients to track status; progress reports that read as status updates (what happened) rather than advisory insight (what it means for the engagement); over-communication in some engagements creating noise; under-communication in others creating client concern. EXCLUDES: scope changes and contract amendments arising from client communication (Scope Change Negotiation Straining Client Relationships); engagement closeout and final handover reporting (Engagement Closeout and Client Handover); external communication and publications (Communications & Publishing).

pain_salience: Creates client anxiety and trust erosion during delivery
Felt unevenly — problem severity depends on individual advisor discipline

painPoints.rationale: AI-generated structured client progress reports are buildable and useful, but the pain is moderate — inconsistent communication is also a discipline problem, and generating a draft does not guarantee advisors will send it.

painPoints.label: Inadequate Engagement Closeout and Client Handover Documentation
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: advisors closing an engagement without producing adequate handover documentation for the client — leaving the client without a clear record of decisions made, implementation guidance, or next-step frameworks; final knowledge transfer sessions with client counterparts not being scheduled or being cut short due to advisor reallocation; clients unable to continue or build on advisory work without re-engaging the advisory team; handover documentation that is superficial or missing, reducing the long-term value of the engagement. EXCLUDES: internal lessons-learned capture for advisory team reuse (Lessons-Learned Capture Skipped at Engagement Closeout); impact evaluation of engagement outcomes (Monitoring, Evaluation & Learning); publication of engagement outputs (Communications & Publishing).

pain_salience: Reduces long-term engagement value and client ability to act independently
Felt at every engagement closeout

painPoints.rationale: Auto-generating a structured client handover document from engagement materials is a strong, low-complexity AI application — clear input, clear output, removes the activation energy barrier for a step routinely skipped under time pressure.

painPoints.label: Advisory Methodology Residing in Senior Staff Tacit Knowledge
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: diagnostic protocols, problem-framing approaches, and facilitation methods existing primarily in the heads of founding or senior staff rather than in documented form; junior advisors learning by observation rather than from codified guidance; methodology at risk of loss when senior staff depart; documented practice artefacts that exist but are not institutionalised — not surfaced to team members, not used in engagements, effectively inaccessible even when written. Methodology here means the analytical frameworks and reasoning approaches advisors use to produce advisory work — how they think, not what they hand to clients. EXCLUDES: operational delivery materials for client-facing use — templates, checklists, toolkits, and guidance notes (Implementation Support & Technical Assistance: Toolkits and Templates Not Reused Across Engagements); case study documentation of completed engagements (Difficulty Extracting Reusable Case Studies Without Breaching Confidentiality, in this branch); publication of methodology externally (Communications & Publishing).

pain_salience: At-risk institutional knowledge is a strategic and revenue continuity risk
Felt when junior advisors are deployed without adequate methodology guidance
Would surface in QA failures and succession planning risk reviews
adoption_likelihood: AI can elicit and structure tacit knowledge through guided interview — novel but viable workflow
build_complexity_inv: Value demonstrable via a methodology documentation sprint

painPoints.rationale: AI-facilitated structured elicitation of tacit methodology from senior staff is a genuine use case, but the hard part is getting senior staff to invest the time — the same delivery pressure that prevents documentation also blocks elicitation sessions.

painPoints.label: Practice Development Crowded Out by Billable Delivery Pressure
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: practice development activity of all kinds consistently deprioritised when advisors are fully allocated to client engagements — this includes creating new methodology, refining existing frameworks, reading and engaging with documented practice material, applying frameworks in engagements, contributing improvements to existing practice artefacts, and attending internal peer learning sessions; no protected time or budget for methodology refinement or learning; improvements to practice only happening reactively when a client engagement exposes a gap rather than proactively; the structural failure being billable-delivery pressure, not individual unwillingness. EXCLUDES: the content of what is undocumented (Advisory Methodology Residing in Senior Staff Tacit Knowledge, in this branch); billable delivery itself; formal HR training programmes not specific to advisory practice (People & Talent); publishing knowledge externally (Communications & Publishing).

pain_salience: Structural failure that compounds over time — methodology never improves
Felt by all advisory staff continuously
board-visible
adoption_likelihood: Root cause is structural resource allocation, not information or tooling
build_complexity_inv: No AI solution addresses the structural billable-pressure problem

painPoints.rationale: This is a business model and culture problem — the organisation does not protect time for practice development. No AI tool changes that structural reality; this is among the least AI-addressable pains in the tree.

painPoints.label: Difficulty Extracting Reusable Case Studies Without Breaching Confidentiality
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: government and funder clients requiring confidentiality over engagement details, preventing the advisory team from documenting rich case examples for internal reuse; advisors having to abstract case material heavily, losing the specificity that makes it useful for future engagements; legal review burden when assessing what can be documented; advisors who have obtained client clearance to document a case study nonetheless failing to do so — the documentation moment passes as the advisor moves immediately to the next engagement and no structured extraction process exists to capture it. EXCLUDES: public case study publication (Communications & Publishing); client data management and compliance (Corporate Operations).

pain_salience: Prevents knowledge reuse, compounding across many engagements
Felt at every engagement closeout

painPoints.rationale: AI-assisted case study extraction (drafting anonymised summaries and flagging what requires client sign-off) reduces the effort barrier, but confidentiality constraints mean client approval is still needed — AI accelerates the draft, not the approval.

painPoints.label: Junior Staff Mentoring Burden Concentrated on Overloaded Seniors
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: capacity building of junior advisors in methodology and analytical approaches requiring sustained one-on-one investment from senior staff who are simultaneously managing delivery; informal mentoring happening unevenly depending on which senior an advisor happens to work with; no structured apprenticeship pathway that distributes mentoring load. EXCLUDES: formal HR training programmes (People & Talent), client-facing capacity building (Implementation Support & Technical Assistance).

pain_salience: Unevenly distributed mentoring creates quality variance in junior advisor output
Felt continuously in small teams with senior capacity constraints

painPoints.rationale: An AI analytical coach for junior advisors is an interesting use case, but the highest-value mentoring (political judgment, client relationship navigation) is tacit and cannot be AI-coached effectively.

painPoints.label: No Safe Space to Pilot New Advisory Service Models
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: experimental advisory formats (rapid diagnostic sprints, peer-learning cohorts, retainer advisory) requiring client relationships willing to try something untested; small organisations unable to absorb the financial risk of a failed pilot; no internal mechanism to fund innovation time without displacing billable work. EXCLUDES: product development for software tools (out of archetype scope), research agenda setting (Research & Knowledge Production).

pain_salience: Blocks organisational innovation and revenue diversification
Felt as a structural constraint on strategic planning discussions
adoption_likelihood: Root cause is financial risk and relationship access, not information or analytical capacity
build_complexity_inv: Possible: AI for rapid service model prototyping or simulation, but tangential

painPoints.rationale: The barrier to piloting new service models is financial risk and willing client partners, not information. AI cannot provide safe-to-fail pilots or remove the revenue risk of experimentation; this is not a meaningfully AI-addressable pain.

painPoints.label: No Structured Peer Review of Advisory Analytical Approaches
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: advisors conducting analysis and producing recommendations for client engagements without systematic challenge of their analytical reasoning from peers; analytical quality variation going undetected because no cross-engagement peer review mechanism exists; ad hoc hallway feedback or informal conversation not substituting for structured analytical challenge; small-team norms making peer challenge feel awkward or disruptive when everyone is stretched on delivery; advisors developing idiosyncratic analytical habits over time without correction or calibration; the organisation having no internal process for asking 'is this the right way to frame this problem?' before the analysis reaches the client. EXCLUDES: client-facing QA of deliverables before submission (Engagement Management & Client Relationship: Quality Assurance Reviews Concentrated at Deadline); mentoring of junior staff on analytical methodology (Junior Staff Mentoring Burden Concentrated on Overloaded Seniors, in this branch); external peer review through publication (Research & Knowledge Production).

pain_salience: Allows analytical quality variation to persist undetected
Felt on every engagement where analysis is not peer-reviewed
build_complexity_inv: Value demonstrable in first use — AI peer review of a real analysis

painPoints.rationale: AI as a structured analytical peer reviewer is a good fit — it creates a low-friction substitute that bypasses the interpersonal awkwardness of asking overstretched colleagues to critique each other, and the rubric can be consistently applied.

painPoints.label: Funder-Driven Agenda Drift
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: research priorities gradually shifting to reflect funder interests rather than evidence gaps or organisational mission, staff time spent reshaping research questions to fit available funding, internal tension between what the field needs and what gets resourced, cumulative erosion of agenda coherence over multi-year grant cycles. EXCLUDES: individual grant proposal writing (Resource Mobilisation), client-specific scoping requests (Advisory & Consulting Delivery).

pain_salience: revenue_impact
cross_functional
board_visible

painPoints.rationale: High organisational pain — mission drift is board-visible and threatens revenue — but very hard to build. The core problem is tacit and longitudinal; no standard LLM pattern captures agenda erosion across multi-year grant cycles, and fixing it requires leadership behavior change, not just a tool.

painPoints.label: Insufficient Bandwidth for Horizon Scanning
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: horizon scanning crowded out by delivery pressures, reliance on serendipitous reading rather than systematic field monitoring, emerging issues identified late after peer organisations have staked positions, missed opportunities to be first-mover on policy-relevant questions, reputational cost of being reactive rather than agenda-setting in the field, failure to anticipate which policy questions will become fundable in the next 12-24 months. EXCLUDES: execution of research once priority is set, client-reactive scoping (those are pull-driven, not push), dissemination of existing research findings (Communications & Publishing).

pain_salience: revenue_impact
weekly_frequency
board_visible

painPoints.rationale: Highest-scoring opportunity in this branch. The pain is real and board-visible (missing fundable questions = lost revenue), the input data is entirely public and digital, and the build pattern (monitored RAG digest) is one of the most proven LLM applications. Low integration burden and zero change management make this an immediate candidate.

painPoints.label: Stakeholder Consultation Process Overhead
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: multi-round consultations to validate research priorities consuming disproportionate senior staff time, difficulty aggregating conflicting stakeholder views into coherent priorities, consultation fatigue among government and funder partners asked repeatedly for input, nominal consultation that does not materially change decisions. EXCLUDES: client discovery conversations billed to engagements, MEL stakeholder interviews.

pain_salience: cross_functional

painPoints.rationale: Moderate operational pain — senior time is real but the problem is periodic and not board-visible. Build is moderately tractable (standard synthesis pattern) but adoption requires changing how consultations are structured, which adds friction with already-fatigued government and funder partners.

painPoints.label: Research Partner Coordination Friction
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: aligning agenda with academic and think-tank partners who have different institutional timelines and incentives, negotiating intellectual territory and credit-sharing for joint research, partners prioritising publication speed over policy relevance, duplicated effort when partner agendas overlap without coordination. EXCLUDES: MOU management (Knowledge Management), dissemination partnerships (Communications & Publishing).

pain_salience: weekly_frequency

painPoints.rationale: The pain exists but is contained to research leads and has no direct revenue impact. The fundamental obstacle is that the problem is relational and multi-institutional — AI cannot negotiate intellectual territory or change partner incentives, and any tooling solution requires adoption by external partners with no incentive to change their workflows.

painPoints.label: Opaque Resource Allocation Across Research Themes
painPoints.description: EXCLUDES: grant financial reporting to funders (Operations & Finance), grant proposal budgeting process (Resource Mobilisation). NOTE: Internal decisions about how approved funds are allocated across research themes REMAIN in scope — only the external reporting and proposal-writing aspects are excluded.

pain_salience: cross_functional
board_visible

painPoints.rationale: Strong adoption profile — the inputs are structured digital data and the output (dashboard) requires no behavior change. The main build challenge is integrating 2-3 internal systems, but the pattern is standard. Board visibility and cross-functional reach give this meaningful organisational salience beyond its moderate urgency.

painPoints.label: Grey Literature Access & Quality Filtering
painPoints.description: INCLUDES (addition): barriers to accessing paywalled academic journals due to lack of institutional library affiliation, per-article fees making comprehensive literature coverage cost-prohibitive, reliance on author-request emails and ResearchGate access as substitutes for systematic access. EXCLUDES: primary data collection, the procurement and contracting process for institutional journal subscriptions (Operations), open-access publication strategy for own outputs (Communications & Publishing).

painPoints.label: Communicating Evidence Gaps Without Undermining Credibility
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: framing 'the evidence is thin' in ways that still support decisive policy recommendations, client discomfort with uncertainty ranges and confidence ratings, pressure from funders or governments to project more certainty than the evidence warrants, internal debate about how strongly to claim causality from correlational studies, communicating confidence intervals and effect size ranges to non-technical government audiences. EXCLUDES: copywriting and editorial choices (Communications & Publishing), client objections to specific recommendations (Advisory Delivery), suppression or downplaying of findings where robust evidence exists but is politically inconvenient (see: Navigating Contested or Politically Sensitive Findings), methodological disputes between conflicting meta-analyses (see: Navigating Contested or Politically Sensitive Findings).

painPoints.label: Assessing Cross-Context Transferability of Findings
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: determining whether evidence from high-income settings applies to low-income contexts, identifying which contextual moderators drive heterogeneous effects, clients assuming universal applicability of headline findings, staff without deep local knowledge over-generalising synthesis conclusions, absence of contextual moderator data making transferability assessment speculative, pressure to assert transferability to secure a policy recommendation even when evidence base is geographically restricted. EXCLUDES: in-country primary data collection to generate new local evidence (Primary Research & Data Collection), client-specific contextualisation and recommendation adaptation in advisory delivery, the act of commissioning new research to fill a contextual gap (Agenda Setting / Resource Mobilisation).

painPoints.label: Keeping Synthesis Outputs Current
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: evidence maps and rapid reviews becoming outdated within 12-18 months in fast-moving fields, resource cost of maintaining living reviews vs. one-off publications, staff citing superseded internal syntheses in client work, no systematic trigger to refresh when new landmark studies are published. EXCLUDES: updating client-specific recommendations, version control of published reports (Communications & Publishing).

painPoints.label: Navigating Contested or Politically Sensitive Findings
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: fields where meta-analyses conflict, requiring explicit methodological position-taking, stakeholder pressure to suppress or downplay findings that challenge preferred policy positions, internal risk assessment before publishing syntheses that implicate funder programmes, reputational exposure when consensus positions later prove wrong. EXCLUDES: editorial sign-off process (Quality Assurance), media handling of controversy (Communications & Publishing).

painPoints.label: Government & Institutional Gatekeeping for Field Access
painPoints.description: EXCLUDES: internal IRB documentation and protocol writing process (see: Research Ethics & IRB Compliance Without Dedicated Operations Capacity), partner logistics and travel management (Operations). NOTE: host-country ethics board approval — where a government body must sign off on research in schools or with public officials — REMAINS in scope as a form of institutional gatekeeping, distinct from the internal compliance documentation process.

painPoints.label: In-Country Partner Capacity & Quality Variation
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: local research partners with inconsistent fieldwork standards, enumerator training burden falling on the advisory team, data quality problems discovered only after fieldwork concludes, partner organisations prioritising relationship preservation over candid finding-reporting. EXCLUDES: partner coordination for agenda-setting, logistics and travel management (Operations).

painPoints.label: Fieldwork Cost Disproportionate to Small-Team Revenue Model
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: primary research travel and logistics consuming 30-50% of project budgets, difficulty pricing fieldwork accurately in advance, grant budgets that underfund data collection relative to analysis and dissemination, trade-offs between rigour (larger samples) and budget that are resolved by cutting sample size. EXCLUDES: grant budget negotiations (Resource Mobilisation), advisory travel for client meetings.

painPoints.label: Inadequate Data Management Infrastructure
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: raw datasets (survey responses, coded transcripts, administrative data) stored inconsistently across staff laptops and shared drives, no standard codebook or variable naming convention, difficulty re-using datasets across projects due to undocumented data structures, loss of access to structured data assets when staff leave without handover (raw data files, NVivo projects, STATA/R scripts), compliance gaps with data protection requirements for interview recordings and personal data (GDPR, host-country regulations), no data management plan at project outset, difficulty retrospectively reconstructing which data was collected under which consent form. EXCLUDES: tacit knowledge, analytical reasoning, and stakeholder relationship history lost when staff leave (Knowledge Management — Knowledge Loss at Staff Turnover), knowledge management of published and formatted outputs (Knowledge Management), MEL data systems and programme monitoring databases (MEL domain).

painPoints.label: Elite Interview Pool Saturation & Response Fatigue
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: small global pool of senior government and funder officials being interviewed repeatedly by multiple organisations, declining response rates from over-solicited experts, interviewees giving rehearsed responses that add little new information, reciprocal expectation management when declining to share findings with interview subjects. EXCLUDES: stakeholder consultation for agenda-setting, client discovery conversations in advisory delivery.

painPoints.label: Premature Framework Publication Under Funder Pressure
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: frameworks published before adequate iteration with practitioners, grant timelines forcing release of half-formed typologies to meet deliverable deadlines, frameworks that gain public traction before internal validation is complete and are then difficult to retract or revise, peer feedback arriving after public launch. EXCLUDES: editorial production timelines (Communications & Publishing), client-facing framework presentation (Advisory Delivery).

painPoints.label: Framework Reuse Without Attribution or Adaptation Credit
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: partner organisations and consultants using internally developed frameworks without citation, frameworks being adapted by others in ways that distort the original logic and are attributed back to the originating organisation, inability to track how frameworks propagate through the field, reputational risk when a misapplied framework produces poor policy outcomes. EXCLUDES: IP protection and legal matters (Operations), publishing frameworks externally (Communications), internal tracking of how own frameworks are reused across own engagements (Knowledge Management).

painPoints.label: Construct Validity of Indices & Diagnostic Tools
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: pressure to produce quantified indices that clients and media can easily cite, trade-offs between parsimony and validity when selecting indicators, weighting decisions that are methodologically arbitrary but politically consequential, index components that are available for some contexts but not others creating comparability gaps. EXCLUDES: data collection for index population (Primary Research), index communication and ranking debates (Communications).

painPoints.label: Internal Adoption of New Frameworks Across Engagements
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: staff defaulting to familiar older frameworks rather than applying newer more rigorous ones developed by the organisation, no systematic process to embed updated frameworks into advisory workflows (e.g., briefing notes, worked examples, template updates), framework knowledge concentrated in the person who developed it rather than transferred to colleagues through structured handoff, advisory outputs applying inconsistent analytical frames because no mechanism mandates use of current frameworks. EXCLUDES: knowledge management and tagging (Knowledge Management), systematic retrieval and surfacing of relevant frameworks at the start of a new engagement (Knowledge Management — reuse-across-engagements), client-facing framework application (Advisory Delivery).

painPoints.label: Framework Version Control & Field Communication
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: no convention for versioning published frameworks and diagnostic tools, partners and government users operating on outdated framework versions without knowing revisions exist, longitudinal research data becoming incomparable when a framework category is silently redefined, tension between publishing improvements quickly and maintaining stability for ongoing partner studies, no mechanism to notify field users of substantive changes to a published framework. EXCLUDES: internal knowledge base version tracking (Knowledge Management), editorial production of updated publications (Communications & Publishing), client-specific framework updates in advisory engagements (Advisory Delivery).

painPoints.label: Senior Reviewer Bottleneck Delaying Publication
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: small senior team being the only credible internal reviewers, peer review queue creating 4-8 week delays on time-sensitive policy outputs, directors becoming a publication bottleneck as the organisation scales output volume, junior staff unable to progress drafts without senior sign-off on methodological choices. EXCLUDES: external reviewer engagement (separate sub-process below), editorial production (Communications).

painPoints.label: External Reviewer Reciprocity & Conflict of Interest
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: external academic reviewers from the same policy community having competing organisational interests, reviewers asking for authorship credit in exchange for substantive review, difficulty finding reviewers without prior relationship to the research team or funder, reviewer feedback that is diplomatically hedged to avoid damaging professional relationships. EXCLUDES: academic partnership management (Knowledge Management), post-publication peer commentary (Communications).

painPoints.label: Inconsistent QA Standards Across Research Types
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: rapid policy briefs, synthesis reports, and primary research applying different de facto standards without explicit policy, no rubric calibration across staff leading to variable output quality, under-review of grey outputs (blog posts, presentations, conference slides) that carry organisational credibility risk, standards drift as organisation takes on more rapid-turnaround advisory work, absence of a defined lightweight QA track for rapid outputs causing sign-off steps to be informally waived, no requirement for adversarial or red-team review of flagship outputs leaving major analytical claims unchallenged before publication. EXCLUDES: editorial style standards (Communications), client deliverable sign-off (Advisory Delivery).

painPoints.label: Fact-Checking & Source Verification at Scale
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: verifying statistics and claims that have been cited third-hand through the literature, tracking down original sources for widely-quoted figures that turn out to be unverifiable, staff time cost of source verification proportionate to output volume, reputational exposure when an error in a high-profile publication is caught post-release. EXCLUDES: plagiarism checking (editorial), data accuracy in primary datasets (Primary Research).

painPoints.label: QA Process Incompatible with Rapid Advisory Turnaround
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: review protocols designed for 3-6 month research cycles being applied without adaptation to 48-72 hour policy notes, review steps being informally skipped when ministry deadlines are incompatible with the standard review queue, no tiered QA system that calibrates rigour to output type and turnaround time, senior reviewers being simultaneously demanded for both in-depth synthesis review and rapid advisory outputs creating impossible scheduling conflicts, post-hoc discovery that a rapid output contained an error that would have been caught by any review. EXCLUDES: deliberate organisational choice to skip review (standards-inconsistency), external reviewer scheduling (external-reviewer-reciprocity), editorial production timelines (Communications & Publishing).

painPoints.label: Knowledge Loss at Staff Turnover
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: tacit knowledge about research context, stakeholder history, and analytical decisions leaving with departing staff, inability to reconstruct why a framework was designed a particular way without the original author, fieldwork context and relationship knowledge not captured in any artefact, onboarding new hires to undocumented institutional positions. EXCLUDES: HR offboarding processes (Operations), academic partnership handover (separate relationship management).

painPoints.label: Inability to Retrieve Relevant Internal Knowledge at Point of Need
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: staff not knowing that a relevant synthesis or framework already exists internally, duplicate research effort because prior work is unfindable, knowledge base searched only by the person who created the content (who knows the filing conventions), tagging and taxonomy deteriorating over time as new outputs are added without governance, knowledge base governance having no funded role or process mandate causing taxonomy standards to degrade as outputs accumulate. EXCLUDES: external publication discoverability (Communications), client-specific knowledge transfer (Advisory Delivery).

painPoints.label: Failure to Systematically Reuse Frameworks Across Engagements
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: no mechanism at engagement scoping stage to surface which existing organisational frameworks are most relevant to the client context, senior staff reusing frameworks they know personally while equivalent or superior frameworks developed by colleagues go unused because they were not surfaced, clients receiving inconsistent analytical frames across multiple engagements with the same organisation, intellectual capital underperforming its development cost because retrieval depends on personal familiarity rather than systematic search. EXCLUDES: framework development process (Framework Development), failure to diffuse framework knowledge during and immediately after the development cycle (Framework Development — internal-adoption-friction), client-facing framework application (Advisory Delivery).

painPoints.label: Academic Partnership Governance & MOU Maintenance
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: MOUs with universities lapsing without renewal because no one owns the relationship administratively, confusion about data-sharing permissions when MOUs are ambiguous, joint project commitments made informally that later create delivery obligations without budget, academic partner expectations of co-authorship not matched by the advisory organisation's publication model, loss of access to partner-held datasets, co-developed research instruments, and contextual knowledge when a partnership lapses without transition planning. EXCLUDES: agenda coordination with partners (Agenda Setting), co-publication logistics (Communications & Publishing).

painPoints.label: Lessons-Learned Capture After Research Projects
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: no structured retrospective after research projects close, lessons from failed fieldwork or revised frameworks not documented, staff carrying informal knowledge about what worked in a given country context without writing it down, cumulative loss of operational learning about research methodology choices across the project portfolio. EXCLUDES: formal MEL of programme impact (MEL domain), client feedback capture (Advisory Delivery).

painPoints.label: Expert Knowledge Hoarding as Indispensability Strategy
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: senior researchers deliberately not documenting proprietary analytical methods, decision-making rationale, or contextual knowledge because documentation reduces their unique positional value, tacit knowledge withholding functioning as informal job security in small organisations with few senior roles, inability to train junior staff in methods whose tacit dimensions are intentionally retained by the originator, nominal documentation that omits the critical interpretive steps making it non-transferable, organisational dependency on individuals persisting even after knowledge capture efforts. EXCLUDES: accidental knowledge loss when staff depart (staff-turnover-knowledge-loss), retrieval failure for documented knowledge (search-and-retrieval-failure), HR and retention management (Operations).

painPoints.label: Event Purpose Drift From Funder Influence
painPoints.description: Funders or sponsors shape agenda priorities away from the original intellectual purpose of the event. INCLUDES: sponsors pushing for product showcases or country spotlight sessions, government hosts demanding agenda items that shift the framing, foundation partners requiring specific thematic emphases as a condition of co-funding. EXCLUDES: legitimate co-design with funding partners, shifts driven by participant feedback during design phase.

painPoints.label: Keynote Speaker Concentration Risk
painPoints.description: Event programming is overly anchored to one or two high-profile speakers whose withdrawal collapses the agenda value. INCLUDES: late cancellations by ministers or heads of multilaterals, inability to confirm speaker commitments until close to event, contingency planning failures when anchor speakers drop, difficulty securing any sufficiently high-profile speaker for anchor slots. EXCLUDES: facilitation quality issues during sessions, attendee no-show and cancellation rates for non-speaker participants.

painPoints.label: Underspecified Event Outcomes
painPoints.description: The event lacks a clear theory of change — outputs and intended decisions are not defined before programming begins, making agenda design reactive and post-event evaluation impossible. INCLUDES: no agreement internally on what a 'successful' event looks like, sessions added to fill time rather than serve a purpose, inability to brief speakers on what they are contributing toward. EXCLUDES: situations where outputs are emergent by design (unconferences), post-event measurement failures.

painPoints.label: Session Format Mismatch With Dialogue Goals
painPoints.description: Agenda defaults to panels and keynotes when the intellectual purpose requires deliberation, working group outputs, or consensus-building — producing passive audiences where active co-creation was needed. INCLUDES: pressure to use panel formats because speakers expect them, difficulty designing interactive formats at scale, working group outputs that are too thin because sessions were too short or too large. EXCLUDES: facilitation execution failures during the event, venue or AV constraints that limit format options.

painPoints.label: Insufficient Lead Time for Intellectual Design
painPoints.description: Programming work begins too close to the event date for rigorous concept development, leaving agenda architecture superficial and speaker briefings inadequate. INCLUDES: late funding confirmation forcing compressed timelines, internal capacity constraints delaying the start of content design, funders requesting events with less than 3 months lead time. EXCLUDES: production and logistics lead time issues, late speaker confirmations.

painPoints.label: Venue and Vendor Reliability in Low-Capacity Contexts
painPoints.description: Events hosted in developing country capitals or non-standard locations face unreliable vendors, infrastructure failures, and limited fallback options. INCLUDES: AV equipment failures with no backup supplier, catering shortfalls, venue air conditioning or connectivity failures, last-minute venue cancellations due to government bookings. EXCLUDES: travel and visa logistics for participants, safety and security issues.

painPoints.label: Hybrid Event Technical Execution Failures
painPoints.description: Virtual and hybrid delivery introduces coordination complexity that overwhelms small event teams — remote participants are consistently deprioritized or technically excluded. INCLUDES: platform instability or poor UX for remote participants, simultaneous management of in-room and online facilitation demands, time zone fragmentation reducing effective participation windows. EXCLUDES: platform selection and procurement (design phase), attendee registration for virtual participants.

painPoints.label: Visa and Travel Barrier Management
painPoints.description: Securing visas and managing complex travel logistics for participants from developing countries consumes disproportionate staff time and risks last-minute no-shows. INCLUDES: invitation letter processing for all participants, visa rejection follow-up and escalation, flight and accommodation booking coordination, navigating airline route complexity for participants from smaller countries, last-minute itinerary changes. EXCLUDES: per diem reconciliation and fellowship stipend compliance (Attendee Management), budget tracking for travel cost overruns (budget-overrun node), fellowship selection decisions.

painPoints.label: Event Budget Overrun and Cost Unpredictability
painPoints.description: Event production costs exceed budgets due to late-stage scope changes, currency exposure, or underestimated line items — with limited ability to absorb overruns on fixed-fee contracts. INCLUDES: venue minimums and attrition clauses, AV cost escalation, last-minute catering additions, FX exposure on multi-currency events, travel cost variance across participant bookings. EXCLUDES: fellowship stipend and scholarship budget administration, post-event reconciliation reporting.

painPoints.label: On-Site Staff Capacity Thinness
painPoints.description: Small advisory organizations run events with insufficient on-site operational staff, creating single points of failure during execution. INCLUDES: key staff pulled into facilitation or hosting duties while production issues go unattended, no dedicated production manager role, reliance on venue staff for tasks requiring organizational knowledge. EXCLUDES: volunteer recruitment and management, facilitation staffing gaps.

painPoints.label: Health, Safety, and Accessibility Compliance Gaps
painPoints.description: Events in non-standard locations or with participants requiring accessibility support face gaps in safety planning, medical provision, and accessibility infrastructure that small teams are not equipped to manage. INCLUDES: security briefing and protocol management for events in fragile or high-risk contexts, accessibility requirements for participants with disabilities that venues cannot meet, medical emergency planning and first aid provision, evacuation planning for multi-day residential events, managing sudden security deterioration during an event. EXCLUDES: visa and travel logistics for participants (visa-travel node), venue sourcing and contracting decisions (which should surface accessibility requirements — a design-phase handoff).

painPoints.label: Participant Seniority Calibration Failure
painPoints.description: Convening the wrong level of decision-maker — too junior to drive commitments, or too senior for working-level dialogue — undermines the event's purpose. INCLUDES: ministers attending who cannot participate in technical sessions, working-level officials sent as substitutes for committed senior attendees, mixed-seniority rooms that constrain honest dialogue. EXCLUDES: speaker selection for keynotes, post-event follow-up with participants.

pain_salience: revenue_impact
cross_functional
board_visible

painPoints.label: High No-Show and Late-Cancellation Rate
painPoints.description: Confirmed participants from government and multilateral institutions cancel at high rates close to event dates, degrading the peer composition the event was designed around. INCLUDES: ministerial schedule conflicts, competing UN or international summit calendars, country delegations reducing travel budgets, political instability preventing travel. EXCLUDES: visa refusals preventing travel, general recruitment difficulty.

pain_salience: revenue_impact
cross_functional
board_visible

painPoints.label: Fellowship and Scholarship Administration Burden
painPoints.description: Managing subsidized participation for fellows from low-income countries generates significant administrative overhead in grant compliance, reimbursement, and coordination — disproportionate to the grant values involved. INCLUDES: per diem policy compliance and documentation, reimbursement processing and dispute resolution, fellow dropout and refund administration mid-process, coordinating stipend disbursement across multiple currencies, managing eligibility and compliance documentation. EXCLUDES: flight and visa logistics execution for fellows (Event Production & Logistics), fellowship selection and eligibility criteria design, post-event impact tracking of fellows.

pain_salience: cross_functional
weekly_frequency

painPoints.label: Participant Pool Echo Chamber Risk
painPoints.description: Events draw repeatedly from the same small network of education system leaders and international education community insiders, reducing intellectual diversity and limiting reach of convening impact. INCLUDES: over-reliance on existing relationship networks for outreach, inability to identify and reach non-traditional leaders, geographic imbalance (Global North over-representation), sectoral concentration (government officials crowding out practitioners and researchers), perception of events as 'club' gatherings. EXCLUDES: funder influence on agenda priorities (covered under Event Design & Programming purpose-drift), deliberate decisions to restrict events to specific seniority or sector cohorts by design.

pain_salience: revenue_impact
cross_functional
board_visible

painPoints.label: Pre-Event Participant Engagement Drop-Off
painPoints.description: Participants confirm attendance but do not engage with pre-event preparation materials, arrive uninformed, and cannot contribute meaningfully to sessions designed to build on shared prior knowledge. INCLUDES: briefing documents not read, pre-event surveys ignored, working group pre-reads skipped, context-setting sessions needed on day one that crowd out substantive dialogue. EXCLUDES: facilitation design failures, session format choices.

pain_salience: cross_functional
weekly_frequency

painPoints.label: Inadequate Facilitator Briefing and Preparation
painPoints.description: External facilitators arrive with insufficient understanding of the policy context, participant dynamics, or session outcomes required — producing technically competent facilitation that misses the substantive target. INCLUDES: facilitators not reading background materials, briefing calls too short to convey complexity, mismatch between facilitation style and room composition (e.g., Chatham House dynamics, senior officials). EXCLUDES: agenda design choices that constrain facilitation, rapporteur briefing.

painPoints.label: Dominant Voice Suppression of Diverse Input
painPoints.description: Powerful participants — senior ministers, well-funded country delegations, or assertive multilateral representatives — dominate dialogue and prevent quieter voices from shaping outputs. INCLUDES: unequal airtime in working groups, cultural dynamics that suppress disagreement, bilateral conversations that pre-determine group conclusions. EXCLUDES: participant seniority calibration issues, deliberate decision-maker roundtable formats.

painPoints.label: Real-Time Synthesis Accuracy and Speed Constraints
painPoints.description: Rapporteurs and note-takers cannot capture the nuance of complex technical and political dialogue in real time, producing session notes that lose the reasoning behind commitments and agreements. INCLUDES: simultaneous sessions with insufficient rapporteur capacity, synthesis quality depending on individual expertise, language barriers reducing note accuracy. EXCLUDES: post-event report drafting and editing, translation services.

painPoints.label: False Consensus in Structured Dialogue Outputs
painPoints.description: Facilitation processes surface apparent consensus that masks genuine disagreement — producing communiqués or working group outputs that do not reflect the actual range of positions in the room. INCLUDES: diplomatic language that papers over substantive differences, time pressure forcing premature closure, facilitators prioritizing harmony over accuracy of outputs. EXCLUDES: post-event commitment tracking failures, attendee communication about outcomes.

painPoints.label: Interpretation and Language Access Failures During Sessions
painPoints.description: Multilingual convenings fail to provide adequate interpretation or language-appropriate facilitation, excluding non-English-speaking participants from meaningful contribution and degrading the representativeness of outputs. INCLUDES: working groups running in English only despite significant French or Spanish-speaking contingent, interpretation equipment failures or absence, facilitators unable to manage multilingual breakout groups, outputs drafted in one language that misrepresent contributions made in another. EXCLUDES: translation of written pre-event materials (Attendee Management), translation and editing of post-event synthesis documents (Post-Event Follow-Up & Synthesis).

painPoints.label: Agenda Time Management Failures With Senior Participants
painPoints.description: Facilitators cannot enforce time discipline with senior officials, ministers, or influential participants — causing plenary sessions to run over and compressing or eliminating working group and deliberation time. INCLUDES: keynote addresses exceeding allotted time with no mechanism to interrupt, Q&A sections running unconstrained because moderators defer to seniority, cascading schedule delays that eliminate afternoon working group sessions, insufficient buffer time between sessions for a dense agenda. EXCLUDES: agenda design decisions that created an unrealistic schedule (Event Design & Programming), venue and logistics delays that push back session start times (Event Production & Logistics).

painPoints.label: Post-Event Report Delivery Delay
painPoints.description: Synthesis documents and rapporteur reports take weeks or months to produce, by which time momentum from the event has dissipated and participants have disengaged.
