painPoints.description: INCLUDES: departing staff take client relationships, project context, and methodology knowledge with them because no structured handover or knowledge transfer process exists, new hires spend weeks without system access or a clear induction plan because no one owns the onboarding process, system access revocation at offboarding is informal — creating security exposure when former staff retain credentials, HR compliance steps at offboarding (final payslip, reference letter timing, right-to-work record retention) are handled inconsistently or missed, the organisation loses standing with clients when a known contact departs without a managed transition. EXCLUDES: the general document management and knowledge findability problem across the organisation (IT Systems & Workplace Infrastructure); offboarding of consultants and associates at project close (Advisory & Consulting Delivery); recruitment pipeline management (Recruiting Senior Technical Staff node).

painPoints.label: Absence of HR Expertise Makes Disciplinary, Grievance, and Redundancy Processes Legally Hazardous
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: there are no documented disciplinary or grievance procedures, so managers handle complaints and conduct issues informally and inconsistently, the CEO or COO must personally manage any formal HR matter (performance improvement, redundancy, dismissal) without employment law expertise, the organisation has no standing relationship with an employment lawyer until a problem has already escalated, ad hoc handling of employment disputes creates employment tribunal exposure, staff are unaware of their rights and entitlements because no staff handbook or HR policy document exists and is maintained. EXCLUDES: performance review cycle management and manager feedback quality (Performance Management node); multi-jurisdiction employment law compliance and local legal advisor costs (Multi-Country Payroll node); individual payroll processing errors (Payroll Processing and Benefits Administration node).

painPoints.label: Board Meeting Preparation Consumes Senior Leadership Bandwidth Disproportionately
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: board packs are assembled by the CEO or COO the week before each meeting, papers are written from scratch rather than drawn from live management information, trustees with limited sector context require extensive contextualisation in every paper, board minutes and action tracking are not maintained between meetings, governance calendar clashes with peak project delivery periods creating recurring crises. EXCLUDES: board strategy content and programme oversight (Organisational Strategy & Planning).

painPoints.label: Operating Across Multiple Legal Entities and Jurisdictions Creates Unmanaged Compliance Drag
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: the organisation has separate legal entities in multiple countries (charity, trading subsidiary, regional office) with different filing obligations, company secretarial deadlines are missed or managed reactively, intercompany transactions and recharges are not documented to the standard required by auditors, the organisation is unclear which entity should contract with which type of client, board composition requirements differ by jurisdiction and are tracked informally. EXCLUDES: grant agreement entity selection (Partnerships & Fundraising), advisory engagement contracting entity decisions (Advisory Delivery).

painPoints.label: Absence of Maintained Contract Templates Causes Delays and Inconsistent Risk Exposure
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: standard NDA, MOU, and supplier contract templates are outdated or do not exist, each new contract negotiation starts from the counterparty's template, legal review of routine contracts is outsourced at high per-matter cost, IP ownership and confidentiality clauses are inconsistent across signed agreements, the organisation has no systematic way to track contract expiry or renewal dates. EXCLUDES: advisory engagement terms of reference and scoping documents (Advisory Delivery), grant agreement terms negotiation (Partnerships & Fundraising).

painPoints.label: GDPR and Data Protection Compliance Is Treated as a One-Off Rather Than Ongoing Practice
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: data mapping and records of processing activities were completed for an initial audit and not updated, staff handling personal data (event attendees, government contacts, research participants) lack current training, subject access requests and data breach response procedures exist on paper but are untested, third-party data processor agreements (DPAs) with SaaS vendors are missing or unsigned — this is the legal compliance failure; the separate IT vendor procurement failure is owned by IT Systems, the organisation is exposed to regulatory risk from event registration and CRM data practices. EXCLUDES: research data ethics and participant consent frameworks (Research & Knowledge Production), IT security controls and access management for SaaS platforms (IT Systems & Workplace Infrastructure).

painPoints.label: Insurance Portfolio Is Underreviewed and May Not Cover Key Operational Risks
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: the organisation has no structured annual insurance coverage review process tied to changes in contract values, headcount, or operational footprint, professional indemnity, directors and officers, and travel insurance policies are renewed on autopilot without a coverage adequacy assessment, policy limits are based on historical premiums not current risk exposure, staff do not know what is and is not covered when incidents occur, remote and international working has created gaps in employer liability coverage that were never addressed when the operating model changed, cyber insurance is absent or insufficient given the organisation's data obligations and client base. EXCLUDES: event-specific liability and venue insurance (Events & Convening).

painPoints.label: Whistleblowing and Ethics Infrastructure Is Dormant and Provides No Functional Reporting Channel
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: whistleblowing policy exists in the staff handbook but has not been communicated to all staff, there is no named policy owner or designated reporting contact, the confidential reporting mechanism (if any) has never been tested or publicised, the board does not receive a regular update on ethics and conduct concerns, the organisation has not assessed whether its whistleblowing policy meets charity commission or statutory requirements, ethics and conduct incidents are managed informally through line managers with no independent channel. EXCLUDES: safeguarding and child protection policies for programme delivery (Advisory & Consulting Delivery, Research & Knowledge Production), HR disciplinary and grievance procedures for employment matters (HR, Talent & Payroll).

painPoints.label: Unmanaged SaaS Sprawl Creates Redundant Costs and Security Exposure
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: software subscriptions have been adopted team-by-team without central oversight, licences are paid for inactive users, overlapping tools serve the same function (e.g., multiple cloud storage and project management platforms), the organisation has no single register of active SaaS contracts and their renewal dates, orphaned accounts for departed staff continue to incur subscription costs because no procurement-side deprovisioning checklist exists to trigger licence cancellation when someone leaves. EXCLUDES: the security and credential risk created by unrevoked access on managed platforms at offboarding (IT Cybersecurity Posture), research database and academic journal subscriptions (Research & Knowledge Production), event management platform contracts (Events & Convening).

painPoints.label: Document Management and Knowledge Findability Are Persistently Poor
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: project outputs, templates, and institutional documents are stored across personal drives, shared drives, and email attachments with no consistent naming or folder convention, staff cannot locate past deliverables when starting similar engagements, version control is informal and multiple document versions circulate simultaneously, onboarding new staff to find existing assets takes weeks of colleague time, no one owns the document management system or enforces conventions. EXCLUDES: financial transaction records, receipts, and accounts documentation (Financial Planning & Accounting), external knowledge products and publications (Communications & Publishing), research data archives (Research & Knowledge Production).

painPoints.label: Cybersecurity Posture Is Below the Standard Expected by Government and Multilateral Clients
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: the organisation handles sensitive government and policy data but has not undergone a formal security assessment, MFA is not enforced across all staff and systems, phishing training is absent or annual at best, government procurement due diligence questionnaires ask for security certifications the organisation does not hold, a breach or incident response plan does not exist in practice, the offboarding process has no IT-security checkpoint to confirm that managed-platform credentials and access tokens are revoked — creating a credential exposure risk distinct from the subscription cost problem. EXCLUDES: the cost and licensing dimension of orphaned SaaS accounts for departed staff (IT SaaS Sprawl), data protection regulatory compliance and GDPR obligations including SaaS vendor data processor agreements (Legal, Governance & Compliance), secure handling of research participant data as governed by ethics protocols (Research & Knowledge Production).

painPoints.label: Remote and Distributed Work Infrastructure Is Inequitable and Inconsistently Supported
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: the organisation has no global device procurement standard — staff in low-income country offices source devices locally with no IT-defined hardware baseline or managed supplier relationship, remote workplace connectivity stipend policies are absent or applied inconsistently with no IT-specified minimum connectivity requirement, IT helpdesk operates only during HQ working hours with no out-of-hours support channel or self-service knowledge base, video conferencing reliability for staff in low-bandwidth regions is unaddressed — IT has no equipment loan programme or configuration guidance, VPN and secure remote access documentation has not been updated since the organisation shifted to a predominantly distributed model. EXCLUDES: HR policy design for flexible and remote working arrangements (HR, Talent & Payroll), event AV and streaming infrastructure (Events & Convening), field research connectivity requirements specific to data collection protocols (Research & Knowledge Production).

painPoints.label: Device Inventory and Lifecycle Management Is Ad Hoc and Creates Support, Cost, and Security Gaps
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: the organisation has no authoritative register of organisation-owned devices and their assigned users, hardware standards vary across global offices resulting in inconsistent performance and uneven IT support complexity, devices are not refreshed on a defined cycle and staff routinely use ageing equipment that is no longer vendor-supported, offboarding does not include a reliable device return and remote-wipe process — leaving sensitive data on uncontrolled hardware, IT has no visibility of which devices are running outdated operating systems or missing security patches, new hires in remote offices wait weeks for devices because procurement is handled ad hoc without a standard supplier relationship. EXCLUDES: software and SaaS licensing management (IT SaaS Sprawl), event AV and presentation hardware (Events & Convening), field research data-collection devices specific to research protocols (Research & Knowledge Production).

painPoints.label: Annual Strategy Is Produced but Not Operationalised Into Accountable Plans
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: leadership produces a strategy document at an offsite but it is not translated into quarterly priorities or resource commitments, team-level plans are not connected to organisational OKRs, there is no rhythm for tracking strategy execution between annual planning cycles, staff cannot trace their work to a documented organisational priority that was set at the start of the year, the strategy is revisited only when a funder or board meeting demands it. EXCLUDES: programme-level MEL and impact frameworks (MEL function), advisory practice methodology development (Advisory Delivery).

painPoints.label: Headcount Planning Is Reactive and Disconnected from Grant Pipeline Visibility
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: hiring decisions are triggered by immediate capacity crises rather than forward planning, the organisation does not model headcount scenarios against grant pipeline probability and expected disbursement timing, new hires join after the project peak has passed, a lost grant or contract results in sudden overstaffing that is expensive to unwind, leadership lacks a shared model of the minimum staffing needed to deliver the current portfolio across restricted and unrestricted funding streams. EXCLUDES: individual recruitment processes and candidate search execution (HR, Talent & Payroll), consultant resourcing for specific engagements (Advisory Delivery).

painPoints.label: Risk Register Exists but Is Not Maintained as a Live Management Tool
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: a risk register was created for board or funder purposes and has not been updated since, risk owners are not assigned or do not review their risks, financial concentration risk (over-reliance on one funder) is identified but not acted upon, operational risks surface as crises rather than being managed in advance, the board receives the same risk register presentation year after year without meaningful change. EXCLUDES: project-level risk management within advisory engagements (Advisory Delivery), reputational risk from published positions (Communications & Publishing).

painPoints.label: Cross-Functional Resource Allocation Decisions Are Made by the CEO Without a Shared Framework
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: competing priorities across advisory, research, events, and fundraising are resolved by the CEO on a case-by-case basis, there is no formal process for teams to surface trade-offs and make collective decisions, staff find that spoken priorities change week to week because no framework governs which trade-offs take precedence, shared services (finance, comms, IT) are pulled in multiple directions without visibility of aggregate demand, the absence of a prioritisation framework creates dependency on the CEO's availability and judgment. EXCLUDES: editorial prioritisation for publications (Communications & Publishing), grant application prioritisation (Partnerships & Fundraising).

painPoints.label: No Scenario Planning Capability Leaves Leadership Unable to Model Strategic Futures
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: leadership cannot produce a multi-year financial and operational model under optimistic, base, and pessimistic grant scenarios, board requests for strategic resilience analysis cannot be met because no modelling infrastructure exists, scenario assumptions are not documented so each planning cycle starts from scratch, the organisation cannot assess the impact of losing a major funder or winning a transformative new grant before it happens, long-range financial modelling is ad hoc and owned by no one. EXCLUDES: annual budgeting and management accounts (Financial Planning & Accounting), grant pipeline probability forecasting (Partnerships & Fundraising), individual project budget modelling (Advisory & Consulting Delivery).

painPoints.label: OKR and KPI Frameworks Proliferate Without Prioritisation, Undermining Accountability
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: the organisation has adopted an OKR or KPI system but has not set a limit on the number of objectives per team or organisation, by mid-year more than 80% of metrics are amber or red with no mechanism to triage, teams report against metrics that do not reflect leadership's actual priorities, the measurement framework is updated at each annual planning cycle but the previous cycle's metrics are rarely retired, leadership cannot identify the three things that matter most for organisational performance in any given quarter. EXCLUDES: programme-level MEL indicators and impact measurement (MEL function), fundraising target-setting and pipeline metrics (Partnerships & Fundraising).
